Music Copywriters and graphic designers need to know about working together to create winning packages. My name is Denise Ford and it is my pleasure to welcome you to this call with our very special guests. Joining us today is Laurie Haller. Hello Laurie. Hello Denise. How are you doing? I'm great. How about you guys? You're fine. Are you fighting the allergies? Yes, I have a couple allergies here but nothing that's going to stop me today. Excellent. And our other very special guest is Donna Doyle. Hello Donna. Hello Denise. How are you today? I'm well. Thank you very much for taking time from your schedule to join us today. My pleasure. Ladies, I'm going to ask you to tell our listeners a little bit about yourselves. Laurie, let's start with you. Some of our folks may recognize you from various boot camps because besides being the master graphic designer that you are, you've also done presentations for us. But could you just tell us a few things about your background as a graphic designer? Great. I own Shadow Oak Studio and I create winning campaigns for shores, nagalogs, logos and branding, just a few of the things I do for clients all over the nation and work with a variety of very talented copywriters. And I also do a lot of speaking with the AWAI and conduct a boot camp every fall. So I have a lot of fun doing all of this stuff. And in addition, I think you have a handful of little munchkins. Yes, three small boys running around the studio and they keep things exciting. Well, and I think this call is going to be exciting. Oh, yes. Thank you for joining us. Kristen told us about the next special guest and this is a person that Laurie has worked with often. It is a perfect match for this call. The goal of the call is to have this conversation about what copywriters and graphic designers need to know about working together. And so one of Laurie's major masterful partners is Donna Doyle. Hello, Donna, again. Hi. Can you tell us a little bit about your background as a copywriter? Sure. I've been writing direct response copy. Gosh, I hate to say it, but it's well over 15 years by now. I really started indirect response working for Prenneth Hall in their business and health and self-improvement division. And they sold a lot of their books through the mail. And that's where I came in and really cut my teeth on becoming a copywriter. And one of my first projects wound up becoming a control for 10 years, which was really an amazing feat and still is to this day. I think they just stopped mailing it, oh gosh, maybe three or four years ago. And then after that, I worked for Medical Economics for the Physician's Desk Reference, also doing their direct mail and writing some direct response copy. And learning a little bit of design there as well, although I'm not a designer by any means, but at least it really exposed me to the different aspects of print production and why it's important to work well with a graphic designer and all of that other good stuff that really formed the foundation for when I went freelance. I became a freelance direct response copywriter in 2000. So I've been doing it for six years. I have clients throughout the country and I've done some controls with Lorry for companies like North Star Nutritionals and Bio-Nutrogenics. And those are mailing and doing very, very well. And also I'm a critic, a copy critic for AWAI and I'm also a member of their copywriting mentoring program. I know, it's wonderful. And where do you live these days, Donna? I live in New Jersey, right by the New Jersey Shore. Oh, that must be beautiful in the summer and a bit cold in the winter. Yes, yes, but actually we're kind of more modeled where I live as opposed to New York City because we are by the water. So we don't get quite as much snow. So that's good. That is good. Well, I hope you have a nice cup of warm tea while we have this conversation. I sure do. I have it right here. How'd you get? Well, ladies, you both mentioned the word controls and for designers and writers, the idea of creating something that has legs, that has long lasting potential is a great desire. And you both have done it and you've done it together. So if we could spend a few minutes talking about the dynamics of effective partnerships, let's pick some situations where you've worked together on a particular project and talk a little bit about the project. And perhaps about how you went back and forth together. What kinds of interactions that you use to create these winning packages. Does that seem reasonable? Oh, that sounds great. You know, Donna, we could start talking about our first project together when we first met each other. Absolutely. That was Weight Guard Plus a few years ago with Agoura. And I had gotten a copy and I had read it a couple of times, getting ready to do my layout. And Don, I don't know if you remember, but I asked the director if I could please contact this copywriter because the copy was so excellent. And I just couldn't wait to talk to this person because I had so many ideas and I wanted to run them past him or her. And so they gave me Donna's name and phone number and I was able to give her a call. And that's really when it started for us. I had already been freelancing for quite a while as well as Laurie. And it was really this client that kind of put us together. Right. And we just hit it off. We were able to brainstorm ideas really, really well. And I was always a great believer that a copywriter should always work with some kind of designer for several reasons. I mean, one is that when I write a magologue, I have things in my head as far as layout, the way I think things should look. And if I worked with the designer, I can translate that into the graphic design so that things flow much more smoothly. A sidebar is not placed opposite copy where it wouldn't make any sense. Whereas if you just let go of the copy and just give it to the client and the client takes care of the design and you're not involved, mistakes happen and mailing spales. And I've seen it in my own experience. And I really feel that being able to work with Laurie on several projects made them so much stronger, so much better, and we have controls to prove it. Right. I think it really makes such a big difference with the brainstorming and the ideas and everything else. Well, that Wacard Plus one at first, I don't know if you remember, but we started it in a one column format. The client was kind of kicking around that look and then we decided to show them a two column format. We ended up even just going with two color. It just blew in black. And to this day, unless I don't have the right data, it's still beating the control two to one. Which we're really excited about. And just the back and forth, I think on that beginning one for us together, I started freelancing about the same time you did six or seven years ago. And I think just the back and forth on that one was our first time that we could really show them how you could work together and make these big controls as well as some of the other ones. We have that Okinawa package that we did together and the same thing with the photo choice, the position of copy. I think the way we interact together as a team rather than one person just doing one thing and going off and the other person going in another direction, that's really what gets us these big controls and then gets us ass back again by the client. Oh yeah. And there are things that we will point out to the client that the client may have totally forgotten about. One is a very recent promotion that Lori and I were just discussing before the call that we did for Dragondore Publishing. And it's at the printers now. But when we were putting the layout and the copy together, Lori's going through it and she's laying out the offer and she goes, oh, what about a speed reply bonus? Right. And I'm like, that's right. I totally forgot about that. And we discussed it amongst ourselves and then discussed it with the client. And in the end, we decided not to go with the speed reply bonus. And since this was a different type of thing that we were offering. But just being able to have that kind of interaction and coming up with different ideas that we can show the client, hey, here's something you might not have thought of. Right. Here are some ideas for what this speed reply bonus might be. And, you know, and it's better for us to be proactive and show that we work as a strong team and alert the client to different things that they might not have even thought of. Right. Especially sometimes when copy can go on for months and months. And Donna and the client and their whole team are reviewing round after round of versions and ideas. I get the final copy and it's just astounding sometimes, you know, that I'll notice something that just won't be brought up because people have seen it a hundred times. And it's really fun and refreshing to be able to go, you know, do you want this title as your premium or your free reporter? Did you want to switch back to the other one? And just that whole teamwork and working together to get those controls in the end is really, really a strong point. Of working together as a team. Well, now with Dragondore, it sounds like that was an idea. Suggesting that came from you about a premium. Is that right, Laurie? Yes. Actually, I was just wondering, you know, I have a variety of quality control checks that Donna and I do on all the things we do together. And I was just wondering where the quick reply bonus was. Normally we do that. And Donna and I just sat down and talked about it and we presented it to the client. And, you know, then in the end, like she mentioned, he told us that we were going to try it as a test this way without one and then test that later after we got the first set of results. So being able to, I guess, Denise, talk freely one another. Donna and I have such a great rapport. She sets the stage so I feel comfortable saying, you know, I tried that red for that headline. It just popped too much. See what you think about this new look. I kind of toned it down to a burgundy and she'll get back to me. Without that kind of bonding or relationship, I just can't see how you could possibly get, you know, these huge controls or access to new clients. I love it when they have a team-making tail tone. Absolutely. And there have been so many times where Lori has recommended me to clients and I have recommended Lori to clients. I mean, Dragondorf is another very good example of that. I had been speaking to John Duquesne, the president, often on for about two years about different projects. When he hired me for this one, he said, now, do you know I need a really good, heavy, experienced, direct response graphic designer. Do you know of someone? And I'm like, of course I do. Lori Haller. And we worked so well together and I gave John a bit of the history and Lori was hired as well. That also happened with a recent project we did for Healthy Directions with D.C. Phillips. I was hired to write a new copy for one of their products and I gave Lori a plug to become the designer and she was. And you know, at the same time, they were interviewing me and looking at my samples and asked who or the copywriters that I hold controls with and really have good teamwork going. And I mean, I gave Donna's name out and that's really how it happens. That's how you get your foot in the door and meet these people. Because I had really been trying to get a piece to do with them for about ten years or that was my goal or my dream. Just hadn't happened yet. It wasn't the right time and this was really very exciting to me to be called by them and have a recommendation from Donna. We had a great time doing the piece. Yes, we did. It sounds like you also work on a variety of projects together, not one particular niche. And that must be interesting. Oh, yes, it is. Like for example, the Okinawa package or what we call the Okinawa package. It's actually an anti-aging supplement that's very high in antioxidants. So with that, you know, you had the whole, you know, ancient secret of Asia, Okinawa theme running through it, which from a design standpoint, Lori was able to have fun with. And I was able to have fun with from a copy standpoint. And really having the opportunity to work on so many different types of products is great because you never get stale. Right. You know, I mean, there's a lot of joint health supplements out there. There's a lot of the same type of products out there. And by constantly moving on to different things, you always can have a fresh approach. And you also learn from things done in the past. And that was another thing that helped so much when you work with a colleague because there might be something that I tried. And Lori can say to me, well, you know, I know in a mailing that I did for another client a year ago, they tried that very same thing and it really didn't work. Right. Or, you know, or, you know what, Donna, we really need a couple of graphs. Can you get together some information so we can have a really good graph on page 16? Yeah, that's what's great is that Donna just comes right back and says, you know, either, yeah, a graph would be great there and here's the data or, you know, the graph hasn't worked good in the past or we tried that before. And just that back and forth and using the history or the data and bring it to each client as we progress is just irreplaceable, priceless. And you both have a sense of the whole package. I mean, I thought that was interesting. You both use that. It's not that you're totally focused in your one area. You feel comfortable enough to make suggestions in each other's domains because you're both interested in creating the control. Yes. Right. Yes. And also when I was working way back when on staff, especially at Medic and Prentice Hall, I was in a situation where I actually hired freelancers. And I worked very closely with other designers. So I already knew what to do and what not to do when it came to working with freelancers and other colleagues. And that definitely translates into one of the reasons why Lori and I work together so well now because I understood how important having that design slash copy collaboration is. When you're a freelancer, you're used to working alone. And many people understand the theory that if you can increase your income and your productivity by working with a partner. But can you both talk to us about ways to do that? How can you work with a partner and what kinds of things working together produce a better scenario for you than working alone? Like communication suggestions, communication tools, and how presenting yourself as a team maybe commands higher fees or something? Well, I definitely think it commands higher fees because the client understands that they are getting two extremely experienced people that are giving their direct mail magologue the best shot it could have. Unfortunately, there's a lot of clients out there that will maybe pay a fee on copy, but they'll chintz on the design. And vice versa. And it's usually, from what I can see, it's usually a mistake. In my experience, there was a client that I had that I gave the copy to. It was for an immunity product. I had no input on the design whatsoever. I didn't see it laid out until I actually got the printed piece. And I knew the minute I looked at this that it was going to fail. I knew it just because of the way the design was. And sure enough, it failed miserably. And now I make it with this client that I say, look, I need to have input on the design. There are certain ways that I wrote this, and I need to be able to translate that to your designer. And also, too, when you work freelance, as Laurie, I'm sure, because I test, you work in a vacuum. And having that communication and collaboration with somebody else is so important, and it really helps us, too, because we're able to really bounce ideas across. And I try to communicate that into things I do with clients, as well. Right. And what I think we also offer them, another reason why we can really command hire fees is it's one-stop shopping, really, with us together. If they need a new report cover, because at the last minute they decide, you know what, we just got the test results in. And on this deadline, that report on the other test did not work. Donna, can you come up with a quick new report cover? Laurie, can you design it? Put it in there? We can take care of everything. There's no worry. They don't have to worry about missing their deadline. We're right on top of it together. And it's less for the client to worry about. Yes. That, Donna, is probably one of the biggest things. They can count on us. I know a lot of people will just give us, you know, hook us both up and just say, you know, this is when we want to get it out. We send them the proofs and we give them recommendations, and they can rest assured that we'll meet their deadline and, you know, all of the best package that we could possibly come up with in the end. And there's a lot of times, too, that, you know, with the communication between Laurie and I, there's so much that we discuss amongst ourselves that the client doesn't even know about. Right. You know, but we feel that by doing that, we can really go back and say, OK, here's the rationale on why things were designed the way they were and why we put the sidebar here. And nine times out of ten, they'll listen and say, OK, that makes a lot of sense. Right, because we have went back to our history or our notes and we're bringing in the knowledge together to help them make these decisions. And some people don't even know, you know, at certain places, if they're busy or rushed or they have a lot of other projects, they don't even have the time to go over all those checkpoints that we kind of do for them. So it really works out well. And I guess you're saying that if you develop your skills as a writer or designer, there are situations where a client will turn to you and say, do you know a designer? Do you know a copywriter? Absolutely. Exactly. And that's how your income increases as well. Right, and your client's stable. I mean, I have just increased my client list tremendously, hooking up with meeting and working with Donna and other copywriters in this exact way, meeting them, either at boot camp or other places, forums, and then working together on a small project perhaps, then going on to another project for a person. Then somebody else hears about the name and it just works out really good for us. Yeah, I have found, interestingly enough, that with some people that may have approached me regarding a future project, they'd say, you know, I hear your name all over the place. And I'm like, really? It's nice knowing that. But I think that's where the personal recommendations get you. And I think that's where, you know, Lori recommending me and me recommending Lori. And we have some really good controls that are still mailing that we could say, yes, we worked on these together. They became controls. We can do this for you too. And if they hear also, I kind of keep this in my head the whole time I'm working because I have very high standards for myself and my company as does Donna. But just that whole professionalism and our communication level and how we handle deadlines and the pressure that can come with that, we're very easy to get along with. We're prompt. We're always at our offices and quickly email back. That's another great point along with all the other ones about Donna is that she's always there. If I have a quick question, you know, are we going to test the second headline? What do you think about this? It's not days and days that passes, which would be terrible for our deadline. She's right back with me or she emails me or calls me and we just keep moving forward to that final goal. So that when you're working with a collaborator, you have to be able to collaborate. You have to be set up professionally and you have to be in touch. Absolutely. You have to have those lines of communications open. It's all about mutual trust and dependability. You know, you could be working with the greatest writer or designer in the world, but if they don't get back to you, if they don't give you what you need when you need it, what good is the relationship? Or if they're not easy to work with. You know, that's hard or it makes more pressure on some of these demanding deadlines. And I just think having us as a resource, I think that's why people call us again and again. We're not just, you know, a copywriter or designer. It's kind of a whole one-stop shop and they can depend on that. So folks who are working on their own and haven't yet found their partners or collaborators, it's important to begin to do that, right, to start to develop a network. Yes, absolutely. I've met the graphic designers that I've worked with, mostly through other clients. Some of them, one or two were people that I knew that I worked well with when I was on staff. But mostly I've met them through clients and I've met them through clients only because I actually said, hey, when this gets ready to design, my C includes working with your graphic designer because I really feel it's so important for the writer to give input. And they'll say, okay, great, you know, we'll give you that person's name and when the time comes, you can work together. And it's also a really good test to see if you would make a good team with this other person. Right. Or maybe even trying something out small and then working on a bigger project. You know, Donna, you mentioned that you knew you do. You really bring to the table a lot of knowledge about art and graphic design. Now, you don't, copywriters don't in general, Denise, have to know that. But I kind of pride myself on continuously trying to study copywriting or learning about it or reading certain books and emails and going to the boot camps and sending myself to these things because one, I meet people that are copywriters that we might be able to work together. But then again, I learn different tactics about a headline or the strengths of the clothes or something. No, I'm not going to write the copy, but I will understand the wording or the information that Donna's trying to explain to me when we're doing a project together. And I think that's helpful. And it's extremely helpful from a copy standpoint as well. Now, ladies, let's talk for a moment, because you mentioned this briefly, about those challenging situations. Those times when you're working for a client and you're trying to deliver what their audience may need, but you run into problems, either in communication with the client or in resources or things like that. How do you handle problems? Well, that's the time that that's one of those areas where Lori and I will talk and talk and talk without the client ever worrying about it. We will often try to offset any problems that we can see at the past. But like in any project, things come up that you don't foresee, you know, and sometimes the deadlines are changed or moved up. That's when we really try to work together as a team and try and solve it together. And be really flexible, as flexible as we can. And just remember the goal in the end. The goal is to please the client, one of the goals, and get a winning package and get this to mail on time, because, you know, it's so costly if they miss their mailing. Maybe they're mailing this. Sometimes people don't realize the whole big picture. They might be mailing this test that Donna and I are doing with three or four other packages. And if we fall behind and don't meet that mail date or that printing date, we could cost them so much money. So even though, for example, we might be a little more expensive than other people, or our rates have gone up since we're, you know, commanding higher fees, working together, we can save the client so much money and time in the end and meet these deadlines and help them as things change. How do you deal with a client when you both make a suggestion and the client rejects that idea? How do you go about negotiating or convincing the client that it's a good choice? Well, sometimes you can't. Sometimes they just have an idea in their head. And, you know, I have my rule of three, which is where you suggest something, the same thing, three times, and if they don't go for it by the third try, that's it. You just let it go. You know, so sometimes you can't change their mind. They just have it in their head, and we are paid to please the client. So we do that. There are other times, however, that we are able to negotiate, and that's where our history together can come in, because we can point, Lori can point to things that she's done in the past, and I can as well, where we can say to the client, well, you know, that's a great idea, but, you know, in a package that Lori did for another client three years ago, they tried something similar and it bombed. Or I can say to a client, well, that is a great idea, but I know of a way to make it work even better, because, you know, I know that for a certain mail or they tried this idea and we made it work, and now I can make it work even better for you. That's great. About technology today is Donna can just say, you know, Lori, can you just try this other option and show them both things next to each other? That's helpful too. So they can see it themselves. Yeah. One thing that designers that I work with always love, and I recommend this for every single copywriter, is to buy Adobe Acrobat, not Acrobat Reader, but the full program, Adobe Acrobat, and in there, what a designer can do and what Lori does is send me the PDF of how the layout is going to look, and then they have a tool in there that looks like a little yellow sticky, where you can actually place the sticky where you want it and then type exactly what changes you make, or if you want copy or revised, and it's an invaluable tool. And it saves so much time then faxing things over and, oh, I can't read your fax, or explaining it in the email or wasting an hour on the phone. That, I have to say, was probably the best software investment I ever made. Yeah, I'd have to agree with that. And it makes us a cut above the rest, I think. And actually, I can see how it can be resourceful also for the client if you send him the sample to envelope copies or something. Oh, yeah. And we've done that where the client can see Lori's remarks and my remarks, and he'll add some of their own, you know? Right. And it's helpful for everybody. What do you do when you get a client who says, this is crap? Excuse my French. I mean, what happens when they really tell you it's terrible? How do you respond to that? Well, I would say what I would do is say, well, why is it terrible? What can we do to make it better? Right. And then we just try and improve it from there. Happily, that doesn't happen very often. Thank goodness. The other thing I think we try to do is I kind of set the stage to, Don. I think both of us do, especially in those first drafts of PDF files. You know, for months, they've been seeing black typewriter type on white pieces of paper. These people obviously aren't real visual people or they'd probably do it themselves. It's our goal to show them, give them a vision, and you can't nail it the first time, every time. So one thing that we try to do together is show a variety of looks. Yes. And I always, like I said, set the tone and say, these are three looks. There's three different ways, and we give them a really strong approach, maybe a softer color palette and then a less handsome looking font that might really be good for this particular product. And it allows them to have a starting point. Before that, we had nowhere to begin but with these white pieces of paper and black letters. So I always just say, I've had a couple of times when a client said, you know, I had something totally different in mind. I was thinking orange and purple and really flowery. And obviously, you know, with the copy I hadn't caught on to that. No big deal. If that's the direction you want to take, let me take, you know, a couple hours and put together a couple more looks for you. I'll work through the copywriter and we'll get something right over to you. That's the kind of back and forth Donna and I do. And that really, that's the kind of teamwork that I think they require and they demand. Yes. And with the fees that they're paying, they should expect to get. Yes, frankly, you know, because it is always still about pleasing the client. It's about customer service for lack of a better term, but it is, you know, and they are paying us our fees because they expect the best. So we want to be able to let them feel comfortable that they are getting the best and that this is something that is using tried and true techniques of direct response that have worked in the marketplace. And you both clearly understand the difference between accepting criticism and not taking it personally. Absolutely. Oh, right. You can't take it personally. You can. These are professional comments and we only take them in that respect and just, you know, let me let us think about this. Let us get back to you. Maybe we weren't on the same page with that whole look. Let us try something and that has always seemed to work really, really well. And in the end, you know, sometimes some idea, I've had this happen a couple of times, this totally different idea that someone on the team might come up with after seeing a couple layouts is the winner killer idea. And if you wouldn't have given everybody a chance or shown a variety of ideas or taken the time, you would have never gotten to that nugget of information. So it's very, very important to have this back and forth even if it isn't the best sometimes. That's a very good suggestion and it does sound like whether you're working in a partnership or not, that's an important skill to develop in dealing with a client and feedback. Yes. And sometimes, believe it or not, sometimes the client really is right. Right. You know? I mean, once you get past the, what do you mean, you know, this stinks? What would you say that? And then you just get some emotional distance from it and look upon it just as objectively as you can. You could say, gee, you know, he's got a point. And then that I find improves everything in the piece because then you see other places in the copy that could be made stronger or could, you know, expound on this theme a bit more. And then it just makes the design flow that much better as well. So it's really a win-win for everybody, I think. Yeah, no one, no one in any business wants to work with a Prima Donna person who only, you know, the artist that only will have it their way or the copywriter that's unbendable or whatever for the general good. And so I think that shows them something else about us too, don't you, Donna? Absolutely. You know, we're willing to work together and we will listen. We're very good listeners and we take direction well. That's a key technique. We'll go into techniques in one minute here, but I have one more question. And there's actually a copywriter who was ecstatic to get their first client asked us about what happens when you put a package together, it's tested and it's on. What do you do? Each of you are together. How do you regroup and move forward? Well, after crying. I was just going to say that. That actually happened to us not too long ago, right, Lori? Yeah, we took a little spill. We were pretty pumped up and it was pretty much 99% sure we were going to win this one huge. And see, I try never to say something's going to win really huge and be so confident. I'm sure I jinxed this, but... Well, I think, you know, I gave that a lot of thought and I remember Lori and I were on the phone for an hour after I heard the news. What I really came to the conclusion was that she and I gave it our best shot. We delivered the best package that we could. And there are a lot of reasons why things fail in the mail that have absolutely nothing to do with the copy or the design. One of the problems with this particular product was that it was in a marketplace that was very, very crowded. Right. So they had to really, really compete to get their message heard. Another problem was that they were extremely FDA compliant, very, very aware to the point where my hands were tied creatively in a lot of ways as to things I could and could not say. So that tended to affect the response levels as well. So things happen. Sometimes you never know the reason why a package fails. Sometimes you can point to things that can, but we tried to regroup and we thought of things that maybe could have been done better. I think one thing was the size, Lori, was the size. Right. The format, I think, hindsight, obviously, is so wonderful, as Brian Kurtz has said many times up in the boardroom. But yeah, I think the format, if I would give anything to retest the format again and just see, that could have a lot to do with it. But the other thing you just said, something really important on is that we did our very best and they will come back to us and they already have. Yeah. So they, if we would have said, well, we can't change this photo or this copy because gosh, you're screwing up all the neat, fancy stuff we were doing. I don't care if you want to FDA comply or whatever. Well, they would never call us back. But being helpful and resourceful and working together, you know, they're going to call us back. We're not going to win every single one, but we want to. But they'll remember us, you know, on a good note. Yes, and that we were easy to work with, that we were very flexible to their ideas. And most importantly, that we met every single one of their deadlines. Right. That's good. So you do do a little bit of postmortem. Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. Right. And you try to analyze the situation and then take some analysis from that moving forward, even if you don't get to apply it to that particular product. Correct. Right. And we also learn to work. We deal with other clients. You know, now in the future, if I have a client that says to me, well, I'm thinking of launching a joint product or this hard product. And I'm not sure which one. I can go back and say, well, you know, the joint supplement is a very, very crowded marketplace. It has to be really different. You might want to consider this other one. Or if we have a client that wants to try a strange format again, we could say, well, hey, we have a hit. We have a history of trying different formats and they don't work. You know, and so we do learn from it. Yeah. Let's talk about it. I'm sorry. Go ahead. Yeah. I was just going to tell you quickly, Denise, this one thing that I do that might be helpful to the listeners is I follow up with a thank you note. And I know Donna does stuff like that too. And then as they call me back, you know, I thank them for, you know, giving me the opportunity to work with them, whether it was the first time or the 100th time. But I also, when I talk to them again, maybe the post mortem, they want to tell me that it either won or didn't. I always say to them and also the production staff from the art end, it's a little different than the copy. And but I need to deal with their printer, their in-house art team, their illustrator, their this, their that. I always make a point to send something to each person and, you know, thank them, but also tell them, you know, we're here to service you and help you and be a part of your team in the future. If there's any software, anything that we can do differently, we are dying to know. I'd love to hear about it. And I've even had people tell me that they've bought some new pre-flight art software and they were wondering maybe if we were going to buy it too. And so we went and bought this stuff and it just seems like it is a good follow-up and it lets them know that you mean business. Yeah. That's good. And that's part of the techniques for keeping your skills sharp, that kind of communication process, isn't it? Right. Now you've mentioned, both of you mentioned other things like you're available to your clients by maintaining business hours and that it's critical to listen carefully, not just respond and be open to taking direction. Can you give us some other techniques for keeping your collaboration skills sharp? Well, I think it's very important to meet your deadlines and if for some reason you can't, let the client know in plenty of time. That's great. If you think there's going to be a problem, you know, if they expect to have something, you know, May 1st and April 30th, you decide it's not going to be done, don't let them know April 30th. You know, try to schedule things that you can kind of predict. Hmm. I'm running into a little trouble on this. Let me see if I can get an extra day or two and try to let them know several days in advance. Nine times out of ten, that'll be okay with the client because they want to have the best product possible. Right. You know, but you still want to try and make all of your deadlines and be respectful to what they have to say and to also know their product. You have to know their product, know their audience, but you know, that's a lot of basic, you know, copywriter stuff as well. That's a great point, Donna, because a lot of people don't realize how much research a graphic designer needs to do or should do on their end. When somebody hooks Donna and I up for a project and they tell me it's on joints or hard or whatever it is, you know, even before I get the copy, I will go ahead and go on the Internet or go to the bookstore, the library and do tons of research myself so I understand what this copy could be talking about. I read two papers every day. I want to make sure I'm on the cutting edge of knowing what's going on out there in our society, not just in this stuff, but, you know, arts, theater, fashion, all that stuff depicts the colors and tones and themes that we will use in some of these magologs. Yeah, when Lori and I are when we're ready to get to the design stage, before Lori starts anything, she and I will have a very long phone call. Who is the market? What makes this product exciting? When you were writing the copy, what kind of themes do you have? She'll have my copy in front of her and then that's when we'll start brainstorming things back and forth. Sometimes we try to become that person. You know, am I a man that has this hip joint or heart problem? Am I in my 60s? Have I responded to a couple other things? Would I want this report in this product? What do I do or what colors would prompt me to continue reading other than just the cover? And, you know, my goal or our goal together then is make them get to that order form and sign up and give us that order. Those kind of things, you know, we talk about in the very beginning and through the whole process. And another thing too is sometimes what a designer looking at copy will think is important might not be important at all. It might be something else that should be expounded on and stressed a lot more. I've run into that a couple of times. And by being able as a writer to work with a designer, you can lead them in the right direction as well. Right. Because when we get the copy on the art end, we don't know that sidebar number seven is really supposed to be the punchiest, biggest one. Now, Donna's very good because she tells you so much information in the notes and it's just so easy. But that is really important. I don't want to just design to make this pretty piece for my portfolio. I'm designing and creating for a reason. We want to gain access to these people and have them sign up. So I need to think about the copy very seriously. Copy is the king here and not the fancy design or flourishes that I might add just for my own well-being. However, good design is extremely important to making the copy work. So glad you said that, Donna. Yeah, but it's true. It's true. I mean, just looking at the story I told before about that mailing that I knew the minute I saw the printed piece, it wasn't going to work. Right. Because the design was so bad. Reading the letter, it was so difficult to track. There were no photos. Now, if they didn't put photos in because the copy was too long, that's the kind of thing as a writer I could have easily changed. Right. But instead, they throw everything in but the kitchen sank. They didn't treat the sidebars correctly and the package failed. Well, that's what's so great about getting feedback from each other along the way. You know, suggestions about, you know, gosh, this might be too long here, Donna, that I maybe I should shrink the size of this photo. Oh, that would be great, Lori. That way we can get that copy in. And just those kind of things, I think, are really helpful. And the way Lori knows about copy and what makes good copy, I think it's a big, big help to her that I know something about design and production. I think writers should always have a working knowledge of the quark or in design, you know, those software programs used to make layouts and, you know, using Acrobat Professional. And also knowing a little bit about print production. I mean, you don't have to be an expert at it by any means, but just to have a working knowledge of why things have to be laid out the way they are. What's the difference between CMYK and RGB? You know, these are the type of things as a writer you should know, and it's another skill that you can offer your clients. I have a couple of clients that want me to handle their printing as well. Oh, that's great. You know, and if I didn't have that knowledge, that's business that I would be losing. So it really is kind of a nice change reaction. Is that what you meant when you said earlier, you're a full service? Yes. You're a marketing agent and a factor? Yes, I am an effect. Both of us are. I even have on my business card. I think you've seen this, Denise. You know, it's full service, design, concepts, copy, illustrations, printing. People these days with the shortage of income sometimes in these companies, they need to come to you and know, oh my gosh, I need this done, whatever it is. I know Donna can take care of it or Lori can take care of it. I know it's in good hands. I can trust that what I need to get done will get done. Yes. So that's why you have a network of collaborators, people that you can turn to to execute this product. That's right. That's right. I mean, I have done that on many, many occasions and nothing leaves my office until I'm 100% satisfied with it. So I know that in the end, the clients will always get the best possible product that they will get. You know, one good example is web marketing. I've done a lot of email direct response by now, but I don't know a lot about search engine optimization or the best ways to have web marketing, but I have a great collaborator that does. Right. I will give him a call and, you know, ask him to help me out with certain things. So it really does increase your business tremendously if you're able to have several people collaborate with you. And just the partnerships, you know, whether it's in copy or design, illustration, any of those really, really help. I believe I've seen a lot of people at our boot camps come to the graphic design section that had already been to the copy one the year before, and they were really interested in trying to hone their skills and vice versa. I've met many people in the copy writing boot camps that are going to the next year send themselves to the graphic design one. That's very important. That's the kind of stuff I do to this day to try to stay on the cutting edge. And then within that, build these partnerships. And I think one of the things that I really appreciate about the way you work, and this is a very important technique. From the beginning, you state your mutual goals. You say to each other, how are we going to produce a control for this client? I mean, that's a great way to start a conversation, especially if you are working with someone for the first time, because then you're both on the same team. Right. I've even known people that have just met each other, either through a forum or some other means, and just decided, you know, this copywriter got this first job doing a small brochure locally. They met a designer. They emailed them. They said, you know, here's the copy. I've been hired. They said I could pick my own designer. Would you like to work with me? And it can start just like that, Denise. And then you keep working together and showing people your work and your name gets around, and you can start doing these mag logs and these bigger direct mail packages. Is it also useful? Do you find that during your process, when you're working together, you separate, I don't know if you separate, but there are times when you're brainstorming, sort of just coming up with ideas, and then there are times when you're taking specific pieces of the material and critiquing it and taking it apart. Is it useful to have a free flow? Like you said at the beginning, Laura, you have a conversation just back and forth, and then you get into actual nitty gritty? You know, I think that's how it works really well for us as some, you figure, you know, I haven't been in on the 10 meetings, like maybe the client or the other associates or team members or Donna has been. I need to quickly bring myself up to speed. So a variety of emails, or sometimes people send me a packet. Then we had that nice conversation and all throughout some ideas. I'm doing this right now. Some quick sketches of how a mag log might look. I haven't even went to the computer yet. I'm showing some ideas for illustrations that we might want to kick around and talking back and forth, and that kind of stuff is so important. I never just go right to the computer and design the whole thing in a vacuum. There's no way I could even think of getting a winning piece doing it that way. And I don't think it would work well from a copy standpoint. I don't know. There was one thing that we did in our Okinawa mailing. One thing I'm fond of doing is having like a little checklist, you know, that the client gets to compare the product, you know, and how much better they feel after taking it. And I was able to, you know, Lori saw the questionnaire and she's like, oh, well, you know, when I lay this out, I'm going to have like a dotted line around it that people can cut out with scissors. And that's exactly what I wanted. That's exactly what I had in mind. And not every designer, especially if you don't speak with them or have any input to the design will even think of that. They'll just put a line or just box it. But when you have, you know, just a little difference of putting that dotted line makes it so much more interactive. Right. And we try to do a lot of stuff that way. With the premium, the look of the premium, or how we show the product, or maybe a burst on it, we try to make our things stand out. Yeah. Typically, Lori will show like a few covers at first, and then she might show one or two spreads, and then she'll lay out the entire thing. Right, Lori? Yeah. After you guys have had a chance to look at it, they might say, you know what, and sometimes this happens with us. Sometimes it's everybody's intention to start the letter, dear friend, or dear, you know, hip replacement person, or whatever it is on the front. And then I see the copy and I start fooling around with it. And of course, I always show what, you know, what is expected or wanted. But then if there's an idea that comes to mind, I'll just try to go for it. That's kind of what happened with that weight guard plus is that we really, really had our hearts set on one column, and we just kept fooling around with it until it felt the way that the copy read properly. And it just seemed like it panned out in the end, and, you know, obviously by the numbers, it did. Well, I can understand how the two of you have had such a successful working relationship, and I really thank you for your generous time. Before we wrap this call up, is there something you would suggest to our listeners about how they can go about forming these kinds of working relationships? Well, I think as Laurie mentioned, going to things like boot camp is certainly a way to meet other people in your business, whether you're a writer or a designer, and you get to just be able to see from someone's personality if you're going to have a rapport or not. I mean, that's a great way of doing it. Right. So through your clients, if they happen to put you up with a designer of their own, that's another good way to establish collaborations. People that you might have worked on in previous jobs is another thing. One of my best copycheats is my former Baltic print at all. Oh, that's great. And he's fantastic, and I would not be as successful as I am without his input. All of that is so invaluable. And in time, it's just networking, it's making contacts, it's calling up people like Laurie and saying, hey, you know, I'm dying to know a webmaster. I have a client that needs a website design. Can you help me? Can you recommend people? And you kind of branch out like that. It's kind of like the branches on a tree. It works very much the same way. And actually, you had just asked me that about three or four weeks ago. That's correct. And lo and behold, one of our artists from our recent AWI Bootcamp, two of them actually had resources or worked in that department that you were looking for, and I wasn't able to do it myself. And so I gave their name out. And so, you know, they hooked up with Donna. And before that, they didn't, you know, possibly know her yet. I just think it's an endless source of contacts and people. I know you're going to think this is funny, Denise, but even though I'm a speaker at Bootcamp, you know, I always come and listen and talk with people and meet them. And every year I come home with four or five contacts and do work with people during that year, which is very exciting meeting these people from all over the nation. Sometimes they're from very far away places, but not only just, you know, meeting them as far as meeting new people, but other contacts and some work that we can do together. And that's another thing too with the beauty of technology. It does not matter at all where someone lives. Right. You know, there is a web designer I know who's very good and he's reliable and he's based in England. Oh, gosh. And it's great because, you know, he's very accessible through email. He gets back to me immediately. And with that five-hour time difference we have, if my client gets back to me with changes that she wakes to her site, I just email Richard by 10 o'clock, 11 o'clock the next day. The changes are done. That's perfect. You know, so my client's thrilled because it's like, oh my God, your guy is so responsive. And everybody's happy. And it worked out beautifully. Well, one thing we didn't tell everybody is that we have never met each other in real life yet. Yeah, we haven't. I've tried it a couple times. But, you know, I'm sure you have people like this in your life, Donna. And I meant it when I said it. Not only are you making wonderful business connections and partnerships, but also people that share the same passion in your life. That's really fun and exciting when you're working at home or in your own studio on your own. Is to have these other people in your life that interject, you know, did you see this article? Did you know this new software? That can really help you out. But, you know, finding people that share this passion. But we've tried a couple times to meet each other, but it just hasn't worked out. But soon, right? That's right. Absolutely. But you are living proof that you can have a successful collaboration and do that online and via the telephone. I'd also encourage folks, if they're interested, in beginning to build their network of partners to start a discussion on the forum, the AWAI student forum, a member forum. That would be a useful place to go. Lori, can you tell us a little bit about the graphic design teleconference series? Yes. I'm very excited, Denise. Upcoming in May, we're having an exciting new teleconferencing series, and it's a three-part series. At first, we're going to speak with Peleg Top, a renowned designer out on the West Coast, and none other than the fabulous Elise Benin, marketing mentor. Our second teleconferencing call is going to be with Rob Davis, just really the ultimate designer in MagLogs and Direct Mail today. We're going to spend some time with him learning about his secrets to success. And then the final one is with Sandy Franks from Agora, who kind of gives you an inside look at what goes on on these big publishing companies, what their needs are, and what can you do to make yourself really stand out and get those top jobs. So it's really exciting for me, Denise. I'm looking forward to it. And that's wonderful. And any folks who are interested in that, you can certainly find out more information about that online. Yes, and I also have the number you can call. It's 866-879-2924. And the online connection, Denise, is the www.thedesignerslife.com and then forward slash reserve my spot. The designerslife.com forward slash reserve my spot. Thank you, Laurie. That's great. Donna, I do have one quick thing before we sign off here.
